data.title: 5 Top Stocks For The Global Rotation
data.description:
International Stocks Outperforming U.S. Equities

As geopolitical tensions present investors with a challenging environment, diversification across sectors and assets can help stabilize portfolios for the long run, in addition to broader geographic exposure. International stocks offer a solid opportunity to spread risk as U.S. stocks underperform while trading at historically elevated valuations.

Around 30 country ETFs have outperformed the S&P 500 in the past year across multiple regions, signaling broad geographic participation, with gains powered by key structural tailwinds and sector trends. Metals, energy, and mining stocks have driven strong performance in Latin America and South Africa, while tech, industrial, and financial stocks have supported gains in Asia and Europe.

Heightened geopolitical tensions in the Middle East have added volatility to global commodity markets, particularly for metals and energy stocks. Prices for these resources can move sharply in response to supply disruptions, shifting expectations around global growth, and changes in investor sentiment. This is why it's important that investors interested in gaining exposure to these markets ensure stocks have solid underlying fundamentals.

How I Chose Top Global Stocks

Using the Seeking Alpha Stock Screener, I filtered for Strong Buy stocks outside the United States but focused on geographic diversity, pulling together a basket of five companies with operations spanning Latin America, Asia, Europe, Africa, and Canada. The basket includes stocks in the metal mining and energy sectors, which have experienced significant volatility. Investors should assess their risk tolerance, as these types of investments are not for everyone - share prices can swing dramatically based on factors beyond each company’s control.

Seeking Alpha Quant Ratings are generated by a proprietary model that analyzes more than 100 metrics for each stock relative to sector peers and grades them across five factors: Growth, Value, Profitability, Earnings Revisions, and Momentum.

1. Nexa Resources S.A. (NEXA)

  • Market Capitalization: $1.51B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Materials

  • Industry: Diversified Metals and Mining

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 3/6/2026): 10 out of 279

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 3/6/2026): 2 out of 60

A new member of the PRO Quant Portfolio, Nexa is a major zinc producer, accounting for about 4% of global production, with mining and smelting operations across Brazil and Peru. NEXA commands a dominant market position, accounting for more than 80% of the Latin American zinc metal market, enabling it to sell at premium prices and maintain a competitive edge in cost efficiency.

With vertically integrated operations, Nexa is well positioned to exploit structural demand tailwinds driven by the global energy transition, with zinc increasingly listed as a critical mineral by the world’s largest economies. The stock has been hit in the Iran war selloff, but is now trading at an even more attractive valuation, and is likely to capitalize on rising commodity prices.

NEXA vs. S&P 500 Materials Sector (XLB): 1Y Price Return

Structural concentrate tightness and resilient demand supported zinc prices, driving Nexa’s annual revenue up 9% to $3B in FY 2025. Strong prices, operating leverage, execution, and byproduct contribution helped push Q4 margins to 33% vs. 27% in Q4 2024. Although annual zinc concentrate production slipped by 3% YoY to 316Kt, due to operational constraints impacting grades and volumes earlier in the year, output bounced back soundly, up 9% YoY in the fourth quarter. Nexa’s production profile also offers exposure to rising base and precious metal prices - in 2025, it produced 11M pounds of silver and 33Kt of copper.

Looking ahead, Nexa’s midpoint guidance for zinc production is at ~335Ktpa for 2026, while the company expects tight inventories and rising demand to support metal price levels. Backed by bullish earnings revisions, Nexa’s EPS is expected to soar by 122% in FY 2026, according to consensus estimates. Strong return on equity and expanding EBITDA margins support Nexa’s ‘B’ Profitability Grade.

Despite exceptional momentum, Nexa trades at a mere 6x earnings vs. the sector’s 16x, while price/sales and price/cash flow multiples are at significant discounts to the sector. A high-momentum Latin American metal mining stock, supported by powerful industry tailwinds, offers investors solid international exposure.

2. China Yuchai International Limited (CYD)

  • Market Capitalization: $1.53B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Industrials

  • Industry: Construction Machinery & Heavy Transportation Equipment

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 3/6/2026): 18 out of 611

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 3/6/2026): 1 out of 28

Another PQP stock and leading powertrain solutions provider, China Yuchai International, specializes in building engines for trucks, buses, construction equipment, and marine applications. CYD has a strong foothold in the Chinese market but is deepening exposure across ASEAN. CYD has been developing hydrogen and methanol combustion engines while launching new high-horsepower products to meet booming data center demand.

CYD vs. S&P 500 Industrial Sector (XLI): 1Y Price Return

Better-than-expected truck and bus engine sales, which significantly outpaced the market, drove revenue up 28% to $3.5B in FY 2025, while operating profit surged 82.7% to $155.2M, supported by continuing cost reduction initiatives. Sales to data centers increased to 2,000 units vs. 750 in the prior year as surging AI workloads required stronger power-generation solutions. CYD’s ability to expand globally and plans to grow its reach even further support a strong earnings outlook in the next three years.

"In addition to our continued expansion in China, we enhanced our footprint in overseas markets with a strategic agreement in Vietnam, shipped high–quality castings to Germany, and delivered buses powered by Yuchai natural gas engines in Mexico. New partnerships with additional global industrial leaders will further strengthen market access in the future,” President Weng Ming Hoh said in a press release.

Strong financial results contributed to a top-notch Growth Grade, driven by a 44%-plus forward EPS long-term growth rate (3-5Y CAGR) and surging cash flow, operating profit, and return on equity.

CYD’s pullback as the Middle East conflict disrupts global supply chains offers investors an opportunity. A PEG of 0.32, representing an 82% discount to the sector, underpins a ‘A’ Valuation Grade, along with a P/E of 14x. Well positioned for more growth in the high-power generation market, while trading at an attractive price, makes CYD a strong pick for an international portfolio.

3. Centerra Gold Inc. (CGAU)

  • Market Capitalization: $3.68B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Materials

  • Industry: Gold

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 3/6/2026): 14 out of 279

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 3/6/2026): 6 out of 49

Added to the PQP portfolio last month, CGAU offers a potential buy-the-dip opportunity after the latest slide in gold. The company is focused on mining gold and copper primarily in North America, Turkey, and other international locations. CGAU has benefited from recent permit approvals to extend operations at its Mount Milligan mine in British Columbia through 2035 as it advances plans to extend the mine life to 2045.

CGAU vs. Gold Mining Peers (GDX): 1Y Price Return

Beating top- and bottom-line expectations for Q4 2025, strong operational performance at Mount Milligan and Oksut, in addition to elevated metal prices, drove revenue up 33% YoY to $401.6M, boosted cash flow, and expanded EBITDA margins. For the full-year, CGAU produced 275.3Koz of gold and 50.4Mlbs of copper, and outperformed cost guidance while investing in a robust pipeline of growth projects.

Centerra also advanced studies of the Kemess project in British Columbia, which has the potential to significantly lift the company’s production profile. Kemess has a 15-year mine life with average annual production of 171Koz of gold and 61Mlbs of copper and, according to the preliminary economic assessment (PEA), is valued at $1.1B based on after-tax NPV at 5%.

The company’s robust margins and strong return on equity underpin a high-quality Profitability Grade. The profitable growth is expected to continue based on strong forward EBITDA and earnings growth metrics, and nine upward earnings revisions to one downward revision in the last 90 days.

CGAU is trading at 10x earnings, while its PEG TTM is at a 96% discount to the sector. Leveraged to gold and copper prices with significant growth plans, CGAU is a high-performing gold stock worth keeping an eye on for a commodity price rebound.

4. Neste Oyj (NTOIY)

  • Market Capitalization: $21.96B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Energy

  • Industry: Oil and Gas Refining and Marketing

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 3/6/2026): 4 out of 224

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 3/6/2026): 1 out of 18

Finland-headquartered Neste Oyj, a PQP stock, is a prominent player in sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) and renewable diesel, with a network of around 1,000 fueling stations, benefiting from regulatory tailwinds and strategic capacity expansions. Beyond SAF and renewable diesel, the company is also expanding into renewable feedstocks for plastics and chemicals.

NTOIY vs. S&P 500 Energy Sector (XLE): 1Y Price Return

After a rough start in 2025 amid depressed oil and renewable product markets, Neste implemented stricter capital discipline and performance improvement measures that helped boost operational efficiency. Full-year 2025 revenue came in at $22.34B, while adjusted EBITDA rose significantly as higher sales volumes and margins offset lower prices, with strong performance across renewable and oil products, supported by improved utilization.

Long term, the company estimates renewable production capacity will reach 6.8M tonnes in 2027. Neste’s A+ Growth Grade is driven by an EPS forward long-term growth rate of +90% and surging operating profits and cash flow. Meanwhile, positive analyst revisions reflect optimism in its earnings outlook for FY 2026.

Neste offers an attractive valuation with a PEG that's at a 78% discount to the sector. A leader in renewable energy that's riding industry megatrends, Neste's excellent momentum and long-term earnings growth prospects continue to drive a Strong Buy rating.

5. Sibanye Stillwater Limited (SBSW)

  • Market Capitalization: $10.12B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Materials

  • Industry: Precious Metals and Minerals

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 3/6/2026): 7 out of 279

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 3/6/2026): 1 out of 7

A PQP member since January, Sibanye Stillwater has footholds in both traditional and green economies, offering exposure to precious metals with long-term lithium optionality. After dipping hard in the precious metals pullback, SBSW is trading at a steep discount to the sector, while facing a potential catalyst if the U.S. imposes tariffs on palladium - a decision expected by mid 2026.

SBSW vs. Platinum (PPLT) and Gold (GLD) Prices: 1Y Return

SBSW has core mining assets strategically positioned in South Africa’s Bushveld Complex, which holds the world’s largest reserves of platinum group metals. Sibanye’s South African PGM and gold operations drove just more than 70% of group revenue in FY 2025, followed by U.S. PGM and recycling operations (~20%), with the remainder from zinc, nickel, and other metal products.

SBSW reported $7.25B in annual revenue in 2025, up from $6.1B in 2024, while adjusted EBITDA grew by 189% YoY to $2.1B. The results were driven largely by higher realized precious metal prices and strength in the recycling business, boosted by the acquisition of Metallix in North Carolina. In terms of long-term outlook, construction on the Keliber lithium project in Finland is expected to be completed in Q1 2026. Nearly 80% owned by SBSW, Keliber has an 18-year mine life and 248Kt of lithium reserves.

Surging revenue, EBITDA, and cash flow growth in the past 12 months drive Sibanye's A+ Growth Grade. The stock is trading at 6x earnings - a 60% discount to the sector, and attractive EV/EBITDA and price/cash flow underpin an ‘A’ Valuation Grade. Also supporting the valuation is Sibanye’s first annual dividend in three years. SBSW paid out 35% of normalized earnings for a dividend yielding around 2%.

Leveraged to PGM and gold prices, with growing exposure to the green economy, SBSW is one of the top diversified mining Strong Buys. Sibanye Stillwater wraps up my basket of global stocks that offer investors geographic diversification in volatile times.

Top Global Stocks for a Rotation from U.S. Equities

International stocks have outperformed U.S. equities, with 30 country ETFs topping the S&P 500 in the past year. The performance comes as U.S. stocks look expensive relative to historical averages. Investors faced with a volatile environment may want to consider diversification strategies across assets and regions. Broad geographic exposure provides investors an opportunity to rotate away from a U.S.-heavy portfolio and capture opportunities emerging across global markets. SA Quant identified five Strong Buy stocks spanning Asia, Latin America, Europe, Africa, and Canada, with strong fundamentals and attractive valuations.

We have many stocks with strong buy recommendations, and you can filter them using Stock Screens to suit your specific investment objectives. Alternatively, Alpha Picks might be ideal if you're interested in two monthly stock picks of the top "strong buy" Quant stocks. Seeking Alpha’s quant ratings and investment research tools help to ensure you are furnished with the best resources to make informed investment decisions while taking the emotion out of investing.

If you’re looking for more frequent investment ideas, we launched the PRO Quant Portfolio in June. It’s a weekly rebalanced selection of our Top 30 Quant Strong Buy stocks, spanning multiple regions and market caps. Happy investing!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Assessing Where Regenxbio Stands After FY25 Results
data.description:
Thesis

Regenxbio (RGNX) has just reported a 4Q25 GAAP EPS of -$1.30, a figure that missed analyst expectations quite a bit by about $0.36. Elsewhere, revenue for the quarter reached $30.34 million, about $15.14 million below estimates despite actually increasing 43% year-over-year. It was a pretty big earnings miss. I think we can put it down to the hefty spending on clinical development and operations, with the company advancing a few late-stage gene therapy pipelines. As you know, this stock has had a rough couple of months. I initiated coverage back in mid-September and was buy-rated into late January.

However, shares peaked at about the $15 range when we got some pretty bad news about a hold being placed on their RGX-111 and RGX-121 candidates. First, we got the upsetting news that they found a tumor in a pediatric trial participant. They found it during a routine MRI, and what made things worse was that it was in a 5-year-old who had received treatment for Hurler syndrome about four years earlier. Upon the news, there was a big sell-off in the market, but at the time, there was still a lot of uncertainty surrounding the continuation of the programs. We then got the news of a CRL, which further hurt the stock going into earnings. So there is a lot to go over here in terms of assessing Regenxbio's position in early 2026.

A hold turned into a CRL

I think at the time, the FDA got a bit worried as there were similarities between the products and the patient populations. Both therapies use AAV-based gene delivery, and both target lysosomal storage disorders. Regenxbio targets a young pediatric patient population here and those with long life expectancy. So the FDA extended the hold to the separate Hunter syndrome program, also just to be safe. We cannot blame them for this. The worry was that there was a possibility of this being a class-wide safety risk.

That can be concerning because, for me, that means the regulators could be worried the brain tumor might not be unique to the Hurler program, but something we don't know about the whole vector behavior, or something like insertional mutagenesis, even long-term expression in the CNS comes to mind. I hate to speculate like this at such an early stage, but it's my explanation for why they took the more conservative step of also pausing that trial.

Now, they have faced a pretty big regulatory setback after the FDA issued a CRL on the BLA for RGX-121, so that's clemidsogene lanparvovec. As a quick recap, this is an investigational gene therapy designed to treat Mucopolysaccharidosis Type II (MPS II), which we also know as Hunter syndrome. RGX-121 is supposed to be a one-time adeno-associated virus (AAV) gene therapy intended to fix the neurological manifestations we see in Hunter Syndrome. So the goal is to deliver a functional copy of the iduronate-2-sulfatase (IDS) gene directly to patients' cells. Also, keep in mind that these cells are found in the central nervous system. So, in patients with MPS II, the mutations are what lead to a deficiency of the IDS enzyme, and thus, we get the toxic accumulation of glycosaminoglycans, particularly heparan sulfate, in tissues, especially the brain.

Since the current enzyme replacement therapies don’t actually effectively cross the blood-brain barrier, RGX-121 was specifically designed to deliver the IDS gene into CNS cells. This is why the market was quite bullish initially, since Regenxbio’s approach, on paper, essentially enables them to produce the enzyme internally and potentially provide long-term correction of the metabolic defect. We also saw the BLA submission get supported by data from the CAMPSIITE Phase I/II/III trial, which showed encouraging biomarker reductions, safety, and functional signals over about 12 months of follow-up. The therapy had also previously received multiple regulatory designations, including Fast Track, Orphan Drug, Rare Pediatric Disease, and RMAT status. All this added to the stock momentum, and I remained buy-rated, not expecting the big setbacks we saw.

But now, despite all these designations and the FDA’s earlier acceptance, they ultimately concluded that the evidence submitted did not provide substantial proof of effectiveness. Hence, the CRL is getting issued in February. Specifically, regulators didn’t like several methodological aspects of the dataset. These included a lot of uncertainty around the trial’s eligibility criteria, particularly whether the enrolled patients clearly represented the neuronopathic form of MPS II rather than the attenuated variant. These two forms progress differently and can complicate the interpretation of outcomes. The FDA also questioned the comparability of the natural-history external control group used in the study. Their view was that differences between treated patients and historical controls may actually weaken efficacy conclusions. Also, the agency challenged the appropriateness of CSF heparan sulfate D2S6. This is a biomarker measuring accumulated substrate in cerebrospinal fluid. The FDA didn’t see this as a surrogate endpoint, which could reasonably predict clinical benefit. This was a big one, since this endpoint was pretty central to the accelerated approval argument. The CRL, however, did outline several potential paths forward, including conducting a new controlled study, in which they treat additional patients with a much longer follow-up, or incorporating some kind of an untreated control arm. But we have to be realistic here and understand that options are particularly difficult in ultra-rare diseases such as MPS II simply because of the extremely limited patient populations.

Implications of the CRL

As you probably already know, conducting additional clinical studies in MPS II isn’t easy and isn't cheap. The big challenge I mentioned is that the total number of eligible patients worldwide is extremely small. Realistically, putting together a statistically meaningful controlled trial will take several years, especially now, since they need stricter eligibility criteria to distinguish between neuronopathic and attenuated forms of the disease. Naturally, gene therapy trials are going to need a lengthy follow-up period, probably multiple years, just to demonstrate durable clinical benefit and safety. These studies are also extremely expensive, and that comes down to the specialized manufacturing of viral vectors and the complex delivery procedures. They then need intensive biomarker monitoring and some kind of global clinical site coordination to access that limited patient pool. On that note, Regenxbio has stated they want to have a Type A meeting with the FDA to clarify regulatory expectations but that they still intend to work toward a BLA resubmission supported by additional clinical/expert data.

Clarity on the serious adverse event

We did get some important updates from management on the call, particularly some clarity on the tumor they found, which is the serious adverse event. So, what we know now is that the company has received the final genetic analysis of the resected tumor from an independent third-party laboratory. As per management, the analysis has identified a rare adeno-associated virus (AAV) vector genome integration event in the tumor cells. And they believe this is associated with overexpression of the proto-oncogene PLAG1. Now, PLAG1 is a gene known to be involved in certain tumor growth pathways. So, to put this in simple terms here, what they’re saying is the viral vector used to deliver the therapeutic gene seems to have integrated into the patient’s DNA at some location that activated PLAG1. This potentially contributed to the development of the tumor.

What’s important here, and also what management kept emphasizing, is that this type of integration event is considered extremely rare in AAV gene therapy. They also noted that the patient had multiple underlying risk factors that could have contributed to the tumor formation. So their view is that the overall benefit-risk profile of RGX-111 and the other AAV-related programs remains favorable. Also, keep in mind that MPS disorders are severe, life-threatening events. Below is from Steve Pakola, the CMO.

“As Curran mentioned, we are working towards addressing the clinical holds for 111 and 121. And in the interim, we have received the final genetic analysis from the SAE in the RGX-111 study. The analysis of the resected tumor was conducted by an independent third-party lab and as we reported, detected an AAV vector genome integration event associated with overexpression of a proto-oncogene PLAG1.”

End of year financials

If we take a look at the latest financial results, the numbers were really quite mixed. We saw some strong partnership-driven revenue growth, but still a hefty investment into the late-stage gene therapy pipeline. Total revenue more than doubled since last year, now up to $170.4 million for FY25. I think we can put this down to collaboration income from its partnership with Nippon Shinyaku and the increased royalty they’re getting from therapies like Zolgensma and Itvisma developed by Novartis. It’s also worth noting that a big portion of this growth came from the $72.9 million in upfront licensing revenue and also about $11.8 million in service revenue from the Nippon Shinyaku collaboration.

Now, despite the strong top-line expansion, operating expenses are still rising rather fast. Research and development costs came in at $228.3 million for the year, mainly due to the trials for RGX-202 and manufacturing investments in sura-vec and RGX-121. So as a result, the hefty spend here means the company remained unprofitable. On that note, they reported a net loss of about $193.9 million in 2025; this was still an improvement compared with the $227.1 million loss we saw back in 2024.

As for the balance sheet, I think the company is still in a moderate liquidity position, but there are some rising liabilities tied to financing structures. Cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities managed to reach $240.9 million at the end of the year, just slightly down from $244.9 million last year. We’re seeing operating cash burn offset those capital inflows from partnership/royalty payments, especially the monetization deal with HCRx. The firm’s total assets also declined pretty sharply to $453.0 million, while total liabilities increased quite sharply to $350.3 million. There are clearly higher royalty monetization obligations here. So it’s a bit of a shift, and it significantly reduced shareholders’ equity from $259.7 million in 2024 to just $102.7 million in 2025. In fairness to Regenxbio, the ongoing trial needs quite high capital, but we need the end result to start showing signs of justification for the spend. Management, however, seemed happy with the current cash sum and gave us a runway expectation into early 2027. So it should see out some of their critical catalysts, importantly, the pivotal data for RGX-202 and the milestones for the sura-vec program with AbbVie.

Valuation

As you would expect with the recent downturn in stock price, Regenxbio shares now trade at an even bigger discount relative to the healthcare sector. It's a reflection of their development-stage profile, but more so the upcoming regulatory uncertainty. Now, since the company is still unprofitable, revenue-based multiples would be most appropriate here. On that basis, the stock trades at about a trailing 2.64x price-to-sales and 2.72x Ev to sales, which are about 24% below the healthcare sector median. Forward multiples would appear even cheaper, with a price-to-sales ratio around 1.72x and an EV to sales ratio at about 1.79x. These are discounts of over 45% to 50% compared with the sector averages. The lower revenue multiples clearly suggest that the market here is assigning a pretty modest valuation to the company’s partnership-driven revenue base, even though it’s growing.

I think it’s safe to say that the big discount here is mainly a result of clinical/regulatory risk, particularly after the recent clinical hold we saw imposed on them by the FDA. Both the RGX-111 and RGX-121 gene therapy programs had safety concerns related to a CNS tumor case. So this definitely increases uncertainty around one of their key rare-disease assets and especially the regulatory timeline. So the market seems to be pricing in elevated pipeline risk here and potential delays in approvals. There’s also the concern that the future development cost could rise if additional studies are needed. So even with Regenxbio having a solid cash position and several upcoming clinical catalysts, this valuation is discounting a lot.

Potential upside from RGX-202

Among all the bad news, we should focus a bit on some of the upcoming catalysts for Regenxbio in 2026. Now, since the stock has sold off so much in recent times, I feel that expectations for the RGX-202 program aren’t as high as they used to be. Particularly as the market could potentially be spilling over the risks seen with RGX-111, since they share a somewhat similar mechanism. As a reminder, RGX-202 is an investigational gene therapy for DMD. Specifically, it’s designed as a one-time AAV gene therapy to deliver a microdystrophin gene. In the case of DMD, this is a shortened but still functional version of the dystrophin protein that is missing in patients. Again, this is due to mutations, this time in the dystrophin gene. The theory is that if they can enable muscle cells to produce microdystrophin, the therapy could potentially restore structural stability to muscle fibers. This would then slow or, in a bull scenario, halt progressive muscle degeneration. Importantly, here the difference now is that RGX-202 uses the AAV8 capsid, which differentiates it somewhat from other competing DMD gene therapies that use different AAV serotypes. The AAV8 vector is considered a bit more advantageous here because it has strong tropism for skeletal and cardiac muscle tissue. This is what could potentially enable more efficient gene delivery and later robust expression of microdystrophin.

Risk spill over

As for the risk spillover from RGX-111, I feel the vector integration with RGX-202 is quite a bit lower. That’s because it uses AAV8 and has some different biological characteristics. Unlike AAV9, which we saw used in RGX-111 and RGX-121, AAV8 has a tendency to remain episomal, which basically means it persists outside the host genome rather than integrating into chromosomes. So that factor is what reduces the chance of insertional mutagenesis. Additionally, RGX-202 is delivered systemically to skeletal and cardiac muscle, which are largely non-dividing tissues, further lowering the likelihood of integration events that could lead to tumor formation. Keep in mind their party analysis of the tumor case found that there was AAV vector integration. Below is from management on the call.

“Clonal integration of AAV vector elements into the PLAG1 gene was detected in the tumor tissue. Analyses supported classification as a PLAG1 family neuroepithelial tumor and are consistent with the hypothesis that AAV vector integration at the PLAG1 site contributed to tumor formation. Of note, this participant had a background of factors that could have contributed to risk of oncogenic transformation.”

So if we take the route of administration, I feel this makes the overall risk of oncogenic events significantly lower for RGX-202 compared with the CNS-directed RGX-111 program. However, I also feel the market won't see it that way and will rather discount the RGX-202 program a bit due to the outcome we saw for RGX-121.

Looking ahead

Clinically, RGX-202 is being evaluated in the pivotal Phase I/II AFFINITY DUCHENNE trial. It's for things like safety, but more so, microdystrophin expression levels, and functional outcomes in boys with DMD. Also, the program has already generated some encouraging early data in which they saw robust microdystrophin expression and favorable safety signals. The aim here is to leverage accelerated approval based on biomarker expression, and then supplement it with functional data comparing treated patients to natural history datasets.

As per management, we can expect top-line data sometime in early 2Q26. We want high levels of microdystrophin expression in muscle biopsies, but also evidence of stabilization or improvement in functional measures. So that's things like motor assessments relative to natural disease progression. A positive dataset here could help a lot with an accelerated approval application, and this would help the recovery case for the stock. Shares can now be picked up in the mid-$8 range after the earnings slide, so there could be some upside ahead in the near term. However, my initial thesis, which rested on RGX-121 momentum, despite seeing upside with shares hitting $15, is now incorrect. So as a result, I’ve chosen to downgrade to a hold rating with the ongoing skepticism around the stock.

data.title: Closed-End Funds: My Top 10 Holdings February 2026
data.description:
It has been quite a while since I've shared my top 10 holdings in my closed-end fund portfolio. It would be going back to over a year ago now, though I intended to update more regularly. Time has gotten away from me, and thanks to some readers who had said they were interested in an update piece on the topic, I was reminded to get to it.

More broadly, we last discussed how discounts for CEFs were generally narrowing at the beginning of 2025. A year or so later, and discounts still remain relatively narrow historically, but the average discount had widened by the end of January 30, 2026, from our prior update. It has arrived at an average of -5% relative to a -3.3% discount.

That highlights that, generally speaking, there doesn't appear to be a lot of fear in the market. CEFs can often be quite influenced by overall equity markets because discounts tend to narrow in low volatility periods and widen during times of pullbacks and panics. With the market failing to provide any real significant pullback since April 2025, when the retaliatory tariffs were announced, and continually to hit new all-time highs, I'd say the market has been rather content for most of the last year.

That doesn't necessarily mean that there are no bargains in the CEF space, as there are quite a few outliers that we look to highlight regularly, and that includes several today among my top 10 holdings.

Top 10

My total number of CEF holdings in this portfolio comes in at 33 (34 with cash). That is down from the 39 (prior to that, I had 42), which is quite significant and is a continued part of my overall plan of reducing the number of holdings. This was helped out a bit by the Tortoise Sustainable and Social Impact Term Fund (TEAF) being merged into the Tortoise Energy Infrastructure Corp (TYG). Of course, that would leave another handful of positions that I've exited and consolidated into other positions.

To highlight some of the background on my approach in this portfolio, here is a recap:

With my main CEF portfolio, I tend to be a long-term investor and will hold some positions for years. I do rather limited trading/swapping, but when there is an opportunity, I will take advantage of it. After all, the discount/premium mechanic of the CEF structure is one of the main advantages that can be exploited, especially between swap pairs. Interestingly, we have each one of these three actions that took place since my previous update to touch on.

That includes making moves based on corporate actions, such as sidestepping rights offerings and participating in tender offers. So, it's not a completely passive portfolio, but I could probably best describe it as semi-passive.

With that, this is how I ended up with so many positions; if there isn't a clear reason to sell in terms of the discount/premium or a swap opportunity, then I often just stay invested. I don't mind holding similar funds across different fund sponsors, as that means different managers that may outperform for a while but then underperform for a number of years and vice versa.

Given the reduction in the number of holdings in the last year or so, I had been more aggressive than usual in swap trades. However, this is still a relatively large number of holdings, particularly in CEFs that also often hold hundreds or thousands of positions—so I'm certainly more than enough diversified.

With that, here are the top 10 holdings as of Feb. 24, 2026, and their current percentage allocation.

Several of these names are the same that showed up in my previous top 10 overview. That includes John Hancock Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund (HTD), BlackRock Health Sciences Trust (BME) and BlackRock Enhanced Equity Dividend Trust (BDJ).

There are two names that appeared last time that no longer made the top 10, because they were removed entirely. That includes the abrdn Global Infrastructure Income Fund (ASGI) and Western Asset Diversified Income Fund (WDI)—we'll be touching on ASGI more below.

Some Opportunities

#1 Duff & Phelps Utility and Infrastructure Fund (DPG), and #2 Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund (UTF)

One may notice a relatively high concentration in utility/infrastructure funds, and I admit, it is one area that I tend to lean heavily.

In fact, the reason that the Duff & Phelps Utility and Infrastructure Fund (DPG) has become so large is because of selling out of the abrdn Global Infrastructure Income Fund (ASGI). I had sold out half of the position last year and put that into DPG, then more recently sold out of the position entirely and put half in DPG and the other half in Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund (UTF).

This is because ASGI's valuation was starting to get quite stretched, particularly relative to these other names that looked like better opportunities. As mentioned, I tend to let premiums run quite high before making a swap, and in this case, I could see at least 5 to 10 points of differential over time.

DPG was trading at a premium at one point, but after cutting their distribution, they went to a deep discount—which isn't uncommon to see. For what it is worth, I continue to expect them to be in a position

Since then, it has continued to trade at this deeper discount, and some of it could be warranted. At the same time, the actual underlying performance for the same last five-year period has strongly favored DPG relative to UTF and ASGI.

With that, the outperformance of ASGI in terms of total returns has come via the fund going from a deep discount to a premium.

That said, ASGI could also continue to see its premium move higher, and DPG/UTF see its discounts remain or even get deeper. That is always a possibility and always a risk when it comes to investing.

There are also fundamental differences between DPG, UTF, and ASGI. ASGI is a non-leveraged fund with some exposure to private investments. DPG and UTF are both leveraged. Given that, some investors may feel comfortable not selling out of ASGI but simply seeing DPG or UTF as a current opportunity to put some capital.

#3 John Hancock Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund (HTD)

HTD is another fund that is related to the infrastructure space but also carries a meaningful sleeve of preferred and some fixed-income exposure. However, this fund also looks like a bit of an opportunity today, based on its valuation, as it has been slipping into a deeper discount. That said, this discount has deepened primarily from the actual underlying portfolio performing better compared to its share price. That's the exact type of discount widening we like to see.

The fund last year also lifted its monthly distribution, which still sits at a very modest 6.86% distribution rate on a NAV basis.

Thanks to the deeper discount, the fund's share price distribution yield comes in at 7.5%.

#4 Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Opportunity Fund (ETV)

Finally, to highlight a potential opportunity that isn't infrastructure-focused, we can take a look at ETV. The fund is a blended mixture of the S&P 500 Index and Nasdaq type of exposure—which then applies an overwrite strategy by writing calls on the underlying indexes.

The fund holds significantly fewer overall positions than the two indexes combined, at only around 143 equity positions. They also overwrite nearly 100% of the notional value of the portfolio, but they roll these every 2 weeks and write slightly out-of-the-money calls.

That means they can adjust the amount of the upside cap. However, it still will run into this cap, particularly in strong bull runs, which we've mostly experienced now.

Some of the other EV funds have shown a decrease in the percentage overwrite, and I've been monitoring that for ETV, but that hasn't changed for this fund. At least, not yet anyway, but it is worth continuing to monitor for these potential changes.

With that, the fund would have underperformed rather significantly if looking back at almost any time frame in the last decade. With a bit more volatility taking place in the heavy tech names, this can become less of an issue. That's where they can simply collect the option premium received and provide a bit of return relative to a sideways-moving market.

The fund has also seen its discount start to widen fairly significantly, to a level that can start making it quite competitive against similarly structured ETFs. ETFs won't provide the same opportunity to play the discount/premium mechanism that is naturally part of CEFs' structure. It's also showing a relative bargain against its own historical level.

Similar to HTD's distribution yield getting juiced a bit by the discount, that is worth highlighting here as well. The current rate comes in at 8.25%, with the actual underlying NAV rate coming in at 7.64%. That's the level that the fund is required to earn in order to 'cover' its distribution to investors.

Conclusion

The market has been rather euphoric for a lengthy period of time now, and CEF discounts more broadly aren't screaming bargains. On the other hand, there are still select opportunities out there for investors looking to generate additional cash flow into their portfolio. Though with that said, the last few days have now started to get more interesting. Still, broader equity market indexes are not all that far away from all-time highs.

data.title: TBUX: In An Uncertain World, This Fund Continues To Deliver
data.description:
Thesis

Every year we write an updated coverage piece on one of our favorite cash parking vehicles, namely the T Rowe Price Ultra Short-Term Bond ETF (TBUX). Unlike treasuries funds, TBUX does take small risks via its corporate sleeve, but its yield is also slightly higher. We enjoy this fund due to its robust analytics and performance, and we are going to show you the best utilization in today's macro world.

Uncertainties abound

With the start of the Iran conflict, uncertainties have skyrocketed. Starting with higher oil prices to inflationary fears if the conflict is set to persist for a long period of time. Nothing happens in a vacuum, and if inflation does spike higher we are going to see the impact on U.S. equities as well via EPS reductions. While some debate if higher oil prices are inflationary or deflationary (via demand destruction), we simply like to point out that consumers will buy less items since they will cost more, thus pressuring net income and earnings.

While some investors like to actively play the markets and position themselves accordingly, we are more of the opinion that in complex situations like today's, regular investors are better suited in hedging via a larger cash position rather than buying options or inverse ETFs. One of the cash parking options is TBUX, which we are going to discuss at length below.

ETF composition

The fund represents a classic short term bond fund via its composition:

Its holdings are investment grade, with a very high 27% concentration in AAA rated paper. The second largest bucket is represented by BBB assets, which make up 32% of the fund holdings.

Corporate bonds make up most of the holdings, with the layering in of credit risk providing for the excess spread over simple treasury funds:

Corporate bonds are 54% of the holdings, followed by ABS at 25% and MBS at 8.8%. This fund is very good at identifying short dated bonds which offer attractive risk-reward proposals. From a maturity standpoint we have the following:

  • Duration: 0.6 years
  • Weighted Average Maturity: 1 year

The fund has not slipped in terms of moving duration higher just to create excess spread, thus keeping risk contained. In this cohort of funds choosing the right names and keeping your risk contained are the keys to success.

Risks profile

Let us look at how the name has done in the past few years when it comes to risk metrics. Firstly let us see what the computed metrics are:

The Seeking Alpha 'Risk' tab offers a low 0.8% annualized volatility figure, and an 'A+' risk grade for the ETF.

When looking historically we get the following drawdown profile:

The fund recorded a meaningful drawdown only in 2022 when rates moved higher and corporate bond prices collapsed. At the time the drawdown was at roughly -1.6%. Since then the drawdowns have been kept under -0.5%. Yes, you read that correctly. The name did not record drawdowns higher than 50 bps, even during the April 2025 market risk-off event.

Expect this dynamic to continue, and with rates set to move lower (or stay here if the Fed is forced) the ETF is set to offer a very appealing downside profile. The macro is helpful for this name going forward.

The best way to benchmark a short term bond fund is by looking at its drawdown profile. The last thing you want as an investor is a move lower is your cash equivalent holdings when the market is selling-off. TBUX delivers here.

Yield and performance considerations

As any short term bond fund, TBUX offers a spread over equivalent duration treasuries in order to entice investors. The current metrics for yields are:

  • 30-day SEC yield: 4.16%
  • Portfolio YTM: 4.31%

So the name offers roughly 40 to 50 bps over Fed Funds currently, with a very shallow drawdown profile. Let us see how it has done historically as well versus treasuries and a cohort of peers:

We are comparing TBUX to the State Street SPDR Bloomberg 1-3 Month T-bill ETF (BIL), the PIMCO Enhanced Short Maturity Active ETF (MINT) and the Janus Henderson AAA CLO (JAAA).

TBUX is the outperformer from the cohort, and remember it has almost no drawdowns. Please note how volatile JAAA has been in comparison, while not delivering on the total return aspect. As expected BIL offers the lowest total return from the entire cohort.

We love to see a fund like TBUX outperform its cohort all while having very small drawdowns. These are characteristics of very good long term holds.

Conclusion

TBUX is a short term bond exchange traded fund. The name has a low duration of 0.6 years, all while its holdings are investment grade. The name has done an outstanding job in the past three years in containing drawdowns, with down moves sub 50 bps. In the past year the fund has outperformed its cohort, all while managing its risk very well. With uncertainties swirling in the world, investors choosing to hedge via a higher cash balance are well-served to buy into a name like TBUX.

data.title: Mitsui: Why I Underestimated This Conglomerate
data.description:
I unfortunately ended up underestimating Mitsui (MITSY) when I last wrote about it. I neither bought it when it was cheap, nor did I properly value it relative to what it has been able to perform until now. The company has outperformed the market significantly, as you can see by looking at my article here.

This is something that seems to be happening with a bit more frequency as of late, I've been observing. It's something that I've been paying attention to also, because if I somehow continually underestimate this company or other companies, then a trend of underestimating companies due to overvaluation can turn into a realistic trend of significant underperformance over a longer period of time.

Mitsui is one of several Japanese conglomerates - big companies with a lot of holdings. To say that these conglomerates have been spending long years underperforming, and that this recent surge of performance would be unexpected, would be an underestimation, and a big one. I have rarely seen a company with such a surge as this one.

The best comparison would be something like the surge and drop in Teleperformance (TLPFY), or some of the surging businesses we see in the IT sector.

There is a lot to like about these conglomerates. They are incredibly large - this one has a market cap of almost 16T Yen - they have some of the most diversified operations on the planet. The company's portfolio structure - sectors like mineral and metal, machinery, chemicals, lifestyle, and infrastructure. All of these sectors are businesses with different correlations and end market trends. Evaluating them is extremely tricky. It also means, typically speaking, when one sector is up, another is down. It has typically resulted in, as you can see above, relatively "flat" performance. That changed some years ago.

What was it that changed between 2021 and 2023-2024, when the company surged as much as it did? It was a period when the company went from pandemic recovery to significantly higher profitability than in the past. The record net profits, which rose to over 1.1T yen in 2023, were related in part to rising commodity pricing (energy and metals specifically, not things like chemicals, if you look at the results on a granular level), coupled with strong performances in what are otherwise cyclical sectors for the company (I'm talking about things like automotive and healthcare).

Furthermore, and this is perhaps often somewhat underestimated, a big part was because Mitsui executed on a very significant share buyback program. That's also a major part of the increase in EPS that you see - there were simply fewer shares to "share" that EPS with. One of the more significant buybacks was executed between late 2022 and early 2023, coupled with millions in treasury share cancellations in 2023.

So, let's see what these recent trends imply for the company

Upside for Mitsui is very hard to see in 2026E

The ratings and considerations for Mitsui are very tricky. Valuation, growth, and other factors are the company's weakness; profit is the company's strength. But part of the reason the company is currently up is a dangerous combination of commodity reliance and cyclicality. The reason that Mitsui is up is simply because these markets, as of late, have been good.

I will be the first one to admit that using P/E or similar models to value Mitsui has its weaknesses. A P/E or P/B doesn't always reflect what I would consider skepticism against the company, versus what might just be straight undervaluation. The fact that the company has been known to do aggressive asset cycling or divestments does not make things one bit easier here. These arguments don't just work for Mitsui, but other Sogo Shosha companies in Japan as well, as they are known at. Traditionally, these conglomerates trade at a discount. I believe this is part of why companies like Siemens (SIEGY) split up its operations.

The latest results we have for Mitsui are fairly old at this time - the fiscal year is ending in March, and the latest results for the 9M period were posted in February, about 1 month ago.

This was of course before the Iran war, but I believe we can work with the trends that we saw, and include Iran (and I will say how I believe this impacts Mitsui). To illustrate the importance of the Mineral/Metal segment, these segments saw progress well above 80% against previous-level forecasts. This has the consequence of significantly increasing company forecasts for the full-year period.

Certain parts of the company's operations saw continued weakness against forecasts. This is mostly segments where other companies also underperformed. A good indication is the chemicals segment. Chemical trading is slow. I have covered the current oversupply in the sector before, and I don't see this changing - neither do, as I can tell, others (including Mitsui, read the company's answer here)

The tone of the analyst questions, if we look at previous filings and materials, focus quite a bit on where the company can find new earnings contributions. This reflects, in my view, caution about the company's growth potential. Mitsui has, in some ways, confirmed this estimate. For fiscal 2026, the company has forecasted profit of just over 750B Yen - this being down from over 900-950B Yen for this fiscal. Furthermore, to add to this, the company's report for 3Q (reported in Feb -26) saw a double-digit, 20%+ decline in net income.

This does not mean that cash flow performance is bad. OCF is good, shareholder returns reflect the company's new dividend reality (which I believe is part of what has driven company returns - higher dividend payouts from a Japanese company is, in my experience, quite rare). So, there's a complex interplay of positives and negatives here - though I would argue that at this price, negatives weigh heavier than positives.

Future company projects are focused on future sectors characterized as growth sectors. I'm talking green energy, I'm talking LNG, I'm talking protein - among others. None of these sectors are "bad" - but I argue the visibility for their realistic return is low. This makes, in my view, Mitsui part of the same current overvaluation trend we're seeing globally. Beyond the company's conglomerate nature, I fail to see any significant differentiation that would justify a different consideration.

I would also say, from an analyst's point of view, that Mitsui and similar companies are very hard to forecast. Unlike some of the European companies and US companies in terms of visibility, Mitsui (and others) provide very little visibility on projects (such as asset recycling) until they are basically "done" or concrete. This style of communication is part of Japanese high-context business culture with slow decision-making processes (typically), and it's something one needs to get used to. However, it's also something that slowly seems to be changing as Japanese companies are disclosing more nuanced information.

All in all, Mitsui's coming few years and its results will be determined by a very complex set of assumptions and factors - including but not limited to Crude price, gas price, metal prices, FX, and related factors. It goes without saying that volatility in these factors will mean volatility for Mitsui as well. The fact that the company is intent on further repurchasing shares is clear from its recent communication as well - this means that a reduction in shares outstanding will create performance uncertainty. This is not specific to Mitsui, but to all buybacks. The reason this happens is that lowering SO creates a difference between fundamentals and short-term performance, increasing EPS while (potentially, I'm not saying that this is what is happening here) obfuscating issues in the company's structure. It's one reason I'm not a fan of share buybacks in the companies that I cover - except in very specific circumstances.

Let's look at apparent valuation and upside from hereon.

Mitsui - Valuation going into 2026E

Another reason I'm not positive about company buybacks at this stage is the company's relative valuation. No matter how you choose to view it, the company is currently meaningfully above its 5-year average. No matter really which valuation approach you choose here - and I've used several - you find overvaluation to a degree of at least 9-40%. Conservative DCF implies FV of between 3,800-4,600 Yen. Industry average P/E levels, which put trade distributors, the closest thing to what I would argue that Mitsui is, at about 12-13x P/E, compared to about 19-20x for the company.

It's currently in line with some of its Japanese peers, but I argue that this reflects a general bullish view on these distributors, and not necessarily indicative of actual value, in the same way that a surge in the utility sector, which I have recently covered, does not mean that utilities are necessarily worth a lot more today.

The company has had an undeniably strong performance in what I consider the short term, triple digits in a short time. What this creates, however, is a situation where growth needs to be maintained to justify that valuation expansion. That's what analysts were asking about in the Q&A a month ago or so - what the company can offer in terms of future growth potential. And it's this future growth potential that currently seems tied to what I would consider opaque and potentially risky ventures (risky in the sense that we don't know what's coming, not in the sense that the company has invested in things that are unprofitable).

Two ways to view the company from a valuation perspective. First, we use a historical perspective. This one implies a massive downside based on the historical P/E of roughly 9x, which is below even its Japanese peers. I believe this is due to the company's complex commodity interplay and correlation, which seems to be somewhat higher than some peers.

Obviously, at a P/E of 9x forward, the potential downside is worrying. We're talking potential losses of 30-40% even with dividends and EPS growth.

Secondly, we can use growth-oriented multiples. Because of about 5-8% EPS growth per year, including the impact from buybacks, one could argue for a 15x P/E here, even if this is meaningfully above the company average. I can accept this outlook and this estimate for the time being. The problem is that even based on this estimate, and growth in 2027-2028 (which I consider to be quite uncertain), total annualized RoR is still negative, if barely (about 0.2% per year).

What this means is that for Mitsui to outperform the market in a way that generates market-beating returns for you here (15% annualized, by my requirement), it would have to either grow at more than 2-4x the current estimate to get 15% annualized at 15x, or it would have to trade at more than 21x P/E on a forward basis.

21x P/E would imply a premium of over 100%, and the estimate would imply that we expect this premium to be sustained over time.

I do not see fundamental justification for such a development - and thus I consider this to be an unjustified outperformance that wasn't able to be forecasted.

In my last article, I gave Mitsui a PT of about 2,650 Yen, implying a FV of about 3,400-3,600 depending on performance. A 3,600 Yen FV. The 3,600 Yen PT, which was my high-end estimate, would be a conservative forecast of 11-13x p/E, which by the way is still above the 5-year and 20-year average. I am thus being bullish by giving this fair value. It also implies a cautious outlook on the highly correlated commodities market - which aligns very well my expectations. I would say that I expect for the medium to long-term for the commodities market, especially iron and LNG (also coal, but we'll see) to decline a bit once certain things have played out. The FV also implies a significant - about 10-20% decline in net income, if we play out these forecasts - remember what I said about company expected net income and current EPS forecasts, and you'll see this lines up with fiscal 2026E expectations.

I see no reason to move from my current assumptions or estimates. The current surge does not alter my medium to long-term views - though it does make me "kick" myself a bit for not investing in the company when it was cheap.

Risks are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

While it's impossible to forecast in detail what will happen to these businesses when exuberance is introduced (as I believe is the case here, especially after the recent surge), I remain a supporter of valuation-based investing.

The flip side risk here is obviously that the premiumization and exuberance continue, which is impossible to forecast. If it does, then this thesis at least becomes invalid in the short to medium term - as has happened for the short-term since my last article in September. We do not know, and cannot forecast, how long the market will put things at this level. The best we can do as internationally-oriented value investors is to determine what is attractive and what isn't.

I currently do not consider Mitsui to be attractive.

Thesis

The combination of international operations and an edge in the Middle East in energy, coupled with cheap funding from Japanese loans, makes Mitsui a company worth investing in at the right price. The "right price" in this case, in my view, is close to a 9-10x P/E upside, which comes to roughly a PT of 2,650 JPY. This is unchanged from my previous article - and the current situation in the Middle East and the Strait of Hormuz only changes my short-term view, not the longer-term one.

Because of this, I currently can only give Mitsui a valuation rating of "HOLD", and I wouldn't buy the company here, despite its recent massive surge of outperformance.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is qualitative
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansions/reversions.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of 2,650 JPY/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of March 2026.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: BioLife Looks Strong For Fiscal Year 2026
data.description:
The back-to-basics approach for BioLife (BLFS), which we described in our previous article, is working. The company is turning around in FY25 by going back to its core competence, biopreservation media (CryoStor and HypoThermosol) and the vials (one of two remaining businesses from an earlier M&A spree, together with ThawStar, that ended badly).

After an acquisition spree that led to a huge dilution and a crash in gross margins, the company has now shed all of these acquisitions bar CellSeal, the vial business (which comes in handy for the strategic agreement with QKine, see below). The company divested:

  • SciSafe (for $$73M in cash)
  • Stirling Freezers, eliminating $7.5M in OpEx and warranties (but at a $9.6M cost).
  • CBS (for $6.1M)
  • evo (for $23.5M)

There was some M&A activity:

  • The company bought PanTHERA
  • A strategic investment in Pluristyx

PanTHERA is a developer of novel cryopreservation solutions based on proprietary Ice Recrystallization Inhibitor (IRI) technology.

BioLife bought the 90% of shares it didn't already own for $9.3M in cash and 241K in shares. Traditional cryopreservation prevents ice from forming initially, but during shipping or storage, "transient warming" causes tiny ice crystals to fuse into large, jagged crystals that have a habit of shredding cell membranes.

PanTHERA’s IRI (Ice Recrystallization Inhibition) technology prevents those tiny crystals from growing larger, avoiding damage and allowing manufacturers to use significantly less of the alternative DMSO, which is toxic to patients and cells.

It also enables cells to be stored and transported at higher sub-zero temperatures, reducing the absolute reliance on expensive, heavy liquid nitrogen shipping dewars containers, potentially even allowing for transport in standard specialized freezers.

In late 2025 data, PanTHERA-enhanced media also showed superior post-thaw recovery for very sensitive cell types like NK (Natural Killer) cells, which are notoriously difficult to freeze without losing potency but are one of the fastest-growing segments of CGT therapies.

BioLife is currently working on a new version of its media products that natively incorporates PanTHERA's small molecules. Management has signaled that these next-gen formulations are expected to hit the clinical market by late 2026, from the above-linked PR:

These next-generation formulations are expected to launch within 18 months and have the potential to provide superior cryopreservation outcomes for certain biological constructs, utilize lower concentrations of DMSO while maintaining cryopreservation efficacy, and reduce the need for liquid nitrogen used in cold chain logistics and long term storage.

BioLife bought $2M of convertible notes in Pluristyx, a developer of innovative induced pluripotent stem cell-based products for cell therapy developers.

Pluristyx has three proprietary technologies:

  • FailSafe is a safety switch technology allowing doctors to eliminate transplanted cells if they turn into tumors (a major risk in stem cell therapy).
  • iACT (Immune Cloaking) is a gene-editing platform that makes stem cells invisible to the patient's immune system, preventing rejection.
  • PluriForm is a recently launched kit helping researchers grow tiny lab-grown organs for drug testing, significantly reducing the need for animal testing.

CEO Roderick de Greef (linked PR):

Their recent development of an iPSC-based biological assay for organoid manufacturing dovetails with our interest in exploring biological assays more broadly as a product portfolio adjacency. This strategic investment follows the same playbook as those we made in Sexton and PanTHERA and is a logical continuation of the recent collaboration between our two companies.

Two major clinical-use licenses, one for a neurological disease therapy and another for regenerative medicine, as well as launching a first-of-its-kind transfection kit to solve bottlenecks in large-scale cell manufacturing.

The company kept ThawStar, an automated, water-free thawing system. Together with their CryoStor media and CellSeal vials, it constitutes a single, closed-loop manufacturing process, controlling the last mile of the therapy—the moment the frozen cells are reactivated for the patient.

Another reason they kept ThawSTAR is their recent full acquisition of PanTHERA CryoSolutions. PanTHERA’s next-gen ice-free preservation technology is designed to work in tandem with specialized thawing profiles, enabling BioLife to offer a complete, end-to-end solution for ultra-sensitive cells that traditional water baths would destroy.

BPM Business

The preservation media remain the core driver, accounting for 85% of revenue in Q4 and embedded in 16 commercial therapies and 250-plus CGT clinical trials, constituting a 70%-plus market share (approaching 80% in Phase III trials).

Commercial (FDA-approved) CGT customers produced almost 50% of total revenue in FY25, up from the low 40s in FY24, and this is expected to increase to over 50% in FY26

Successful cross-selling can increase ARPU 2-3x compared to preservation media alone. An important step here is the strategic agreement with UK-based QKine for exclusive distribution of cytokines.

The companies are working to package cytokines in CellSeal vials, a project expected to take six to nine months before contributing to revenue.

Management is evaluating additional M&A opportunities to broaden its offering in the CGT market. An ERP system went live, automating processes and improving controls, in February 2026.

The BIOSECURE Act, signed into law on Dec. 18, 2025, is likely to provide a significant tailwind as it cuts low-cost Chinese competition, like WuXi AppTec, BGI, and MGI, out of the US supply chain.

With FDA approvals tied to specific manufacturing sites and materials, pharma companies have strong incentives to switch to BioLife products to avoid being locked out of federal contracts (like those with the Veterans Administration or Department of Defense) years down the line.

Operational Challenges

There is a 200-300bp headwind on gross margin in the H2/25 due to yield problems with the production of product bags. Management is working to solve these problems, but it requires a 90-day customer notification, and (Q4CC):

And then in addition to that, we have to sell through the higher cost inventory that we have in terms of finished product that's in bags sitting in our warehouse before we'll start to see the impact of the higher yield bags come through, which we expect would be right around Q4 of this year.

In 18-24 months, management aims to replace bags with a rigid container for shipping products to customers.

Finances

  • Q4 revenue was up 20% to $24.8M.
  • FY25 revenue was up 29% to $96.2M, at the high end of the company's guidance (which was already raised twice in H2/25).
  • Q4 EPS of $0.04 beat analyst expectations by $0.03.
  • Adjusted EBITDA for FY25 was $25M (26% of revenue), up from $13.3M (18% of revenue) in FY24.
  • Q4 adjusted EBITDA was $6.9M (28% of revenue), including a one-time $1.3M sales tax true-up gain.
  • FY25 adjusted net income of $6.3M, compared to a loss of $2.9M in FY24.
  • At year-end, the company held $120.2M in cash and marketable securities, bolstered by $23.5M in proceeds from the SAVSU divestiture. Management plans to pay off its remaining $5M FCB debt by June 2026.

Guidance

  • Revenue is expected to be between $112.5M and $115M, representing 17% to 20% growth.
  • Management expects to achieve FY26 positive GAAP net income for the first time in many years.
  • Gross margin is expected to be in the mid-60s, in line with FY25.
  • Management expects considerable operating leverage with a further expansion of adjusted EBITDA margins, driven by revenue growth, though partially offset by increased R&D and S&M investments.
  • Management anticipates up to five unique therapy approvals, one new indication, and at least one geographic expansion in the CGT space over the next 12 months.
  • More than 80% of BioLife’s commercial exposure is related to CAR T therapies. The removal of REMS (Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Strategy) will make things easier for increasing patient access, so this is a regulatory tailwind.
  • Management sees small clinical customers remain somewhat budget-constrained, but large pharma investment and advances in manufacturing automation are favorable backdrops.

Valuation

The shares are still not cheap with an EV of some $920M, the shares sell at 8x EV/S, and, given the recent turnaround, earnings multiples aren't very informative yet.

We think the shares deserve a considerable premium valuation as the company has a considerable moat, growth is baked in (therapies moving through ever larger trials and some becoming commercial), regulatory tailwinds, high gross margins creating considerable operating leverage, with an iron-clad balance sheet to boot.

Bar 2022-2024, the years of the unfortunate M&A spree, the shares were valued higher than today.

Other risks

We already described the bag yield issue above, which is supposed to be fixed by Q4/26, but delays could cause some further drag on margins. There are some other things to look out for:

  • Biotech funding. With half of the company's revenue coming from commercial therapies, the remaining half is still dependent on clinical trials, which are dependent on funding cycles. On the one hand, appetite from investors for Phase 1 trials is waning, but on the other hand, there is a bit of an M&A wave with big pharma buying late-stage biotech companies, and there is some regulatory easing (the new "Plausible Mechanism" framework, allowing faster approval of rare gene therapies).
  • Pressure on drug prices from pricing deals and TrumpRx.gov. While these might squeeze some of its customers, we don't see too much fallout for BioLife here.
  • Given the substantial valuation, any growth and/or earnings disappointment could trigger a re-rating of valuation multiples that could be brutal.

Conclusion

We don't see the shares crashing through $20 unless there's a broad market selloff, as the company is back on track, and given its dominant market share in CGT clinical trials, revenue automatically increases when the trials move through the phases, and some get approved and become commercial.

We expect considerable operating leverage, boosting cash generation and profitability, while the company already has an ironclad balance sheet.

Acquisitions and strategic partnerships like the recent one with QKine can boost ARPU, although we hope management has learned from the previous fiascos with M&A.

The upshot: Buy at $20 (an indicative buy point, not to be taken exactly, but we think the shares are interesting at these levels after spending the past 6 months well above $20).
